coredata.dc:title: Hydrothermal synthesis of nanocellulose-based fluorescent hydrogel for mercury ion detection
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Cheng, Fangchao
Zhang, Shuo
Zhang, Lveting
Sun, Jianping
Wu, Yiqiang
coredata.dc:description: Cellulose nanofibers (CNF) possess excellent hydrophilicity, biocompatibility, high specific surface area and biodegradability, and the application of CNF to fluorescent hydrogels is one of the hotspots in the field of functional hydrogels. However, the rapid and efficient preparation of them remains a challenge, and it is necessary to explore its application in the detection of heavy metal ions. In this study, a hydrothermal method was adopted to investigate the one-pot synthesis of CNF-based fluorescent hydrogel (CFH) for mercury ion detection, which synchronously enabled CFH formation and in-situ carbon quantum dots synthesis. The as-prepared CFH had a complete 3D network structure, and epichlorohydrin showed positive effects on the mechanical properties of CFH. Moreover, excellent fluorescence performance can be achieved through regulating the synthesis process of carbon quantum dots. The as-prepared CFH showed different quenching effects in the Hg2+ solution with different concentrations, and the fluorescence intensity of CFH was closely related to the Hg2+ concentration in the range of 0–300 mmol/L, which showed great development potential and prospects in the fields of fluorescence probes on Hg2+ sensing and detection. This study provided a promising method for the rapid preparation of biocompatible and environment-friendly fluorescent hydrogel probes and ion detection materials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cellulose nanofibers
Carbon quantum dots
Fluorescent hydrogel
Hydrothermal synthesis
Mercury ion detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration of the preparation of CFH using one-step hydrothermal method; (b) Reaction mechanism of CNF and CA; (c) Reaction mechanism of CA and EDA [24,25].

Digital photographs of CFH under visible and ultraviolet light: (a) CFH samples with different reaction time; (b) CFH samples with different CA: EDA ratios; (c) CFH samples with different crosslinking agents; (d) CFH samples with different formulations. (e) Emission spectrum of CFH-2 under 480 nm excitation light.
(a-b) SEM images of CNF; (c-d) SEM images of CFH-16; (e-f) SEM images of CFH-2; (g) FT-IR spectra of CFH-2 and CFH-16; (h) Mechanical properties of CFH samples with different ECH dosages.

(a) Fluorescence spectra at different excitation wavelengths for the decolorized hydrogel; (b) Fluorescence spectra at different excitation wavelengths for the untreated hydrogel; (c) Digital photographs of aqueous solution obtained in the hydrogel decolorization.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of CFH-2 at different excitation wavelengths; (b) Fluorescence spectra of Hg2+ treated hydrogel at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm; (c) the correlationship between fluorescence emission intensity at 458 nm and mercury ion concentration.
Conditions adopted for hydrogel optimization experiments.

head.ce:title: Hydrothermal synthesis of nanocellulose-based fluorescent hydrogel for mercury ion detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Material Science and Engineering, Central South University of Forestry and Technology, Changsha 410004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Material Science and Engineering, Central South University of Forestry and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Material Science and Engineering, Central South University of Forestry and Technology, Changsha 410004, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Processing for Nonferrous Metallic and Featured Materials, School of Resources, Environment and Materials, Guangxi University, Nanning 530004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Processing for Nonferrous Metallic and Featured Materials, School of Resources, Environment and Materials, Guangxi University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Processing for Nonferrous Metallic and Featured Materials, School of Resources, Environment and Materials, Guangxi University, Nanning 530004, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: College of Material Science and Engineering, Central South University of Forestry and Technology, Changsha 410004, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Material Science and Engineering, Central South University of Forestry and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Cellulose nanofibers (CNF) possess excellent hydrophilicity, biocompatibility, high specific surface area and biodegradability, and the application of CNF to fluorescent hydrogels is one of the hotspots in the field of functional hydrogels. However, the rapid and efficient preparation of them remains a challenge, and it is necessary to explore its application in the detection of heavy metal ions. In this study, a hydrothermal method was adopted to investigate the one-pot synthesis of CNF-based fluorescent hydrogel (CFH) for mercury ion detection, which synchronously enabled CFH formation and in-situ carbon quantum dots synthesis. The as-prepared CFH had a complete 3D network structure, and epichlorohydrin showed positive effects on the mechanical properties of CFH. Moreover, excellent fluorescence performance can be achieved through regulating the synthesis process of carbon quantum dots. The as-prepared CFH showed different quenching effects in the Hg2+ solution with different concentrations, and the fluorescence intensity of CFH was closely related to the Hg2+ concentration in the range of 0–300 mmol/L, which showed great development potential and prospects in the fields of fluorescence probes on Hg2+ sensing and detection. This study provided a promising method for the rapid preparation of biocompatible and environment-friendly fluorescent hydrogel probes and ion detection materials.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent hydrogels have been widely used in probes, ion detection and other fields due to their intelligent response to temperature [1], pH [2], light [3] and ions [4]. Fluorescent hydrogels are endowed with fluorescent properties by introducing fluorescent materials, such as organic dyes, semiconductor quantum dots, and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) [5–7]. Among them, CQDs have attracted much attention as promising fluorescent probes due to their excellent fluorescence performance, biological solubility, light stability, and low toxicity [8,9]. At the same time, the hydrophilic groups on the surface of CQDs, such as carboxyl and amino groups, not only enable the homogeneous dispersion of CQDs in water, but also enhance their ion detection functionality [10].

In theory, all carbon-rich materials can be used as carbon sources for the preparation of CQDs, while cellulose has been recognized as one of the best sources due to its low-cost, natural, non-toxic, abundant characteristics [11]. At present, cellulose, in the form of solid powder or fiber, is employed as a carbon source of CQDs and mixed with other precursors, but the mixing uniformity of precursors and cellulose will affect the production of CQDs. The cellulose nanofibers (CNF) can act as excellent carriers and dispersants for the precursors due to their abundant functional groups and hydrogen bond network [12]. Moreover, the preparation of traditional fluorescent hydrogels involves complex processes, including the preparation, purification, and loading of CQDs, and the introduction of CQDs will influence the crosslinking of the hydrogel matrix and finally affect the hydrogel strength. In addition, the matrix material of the hydrogel will also play an important role on the structure, mechanical properties and fluorescence intensity of the CQD-based hydrogel. When CNF is introduced into the hydrogel, the effective cross-linking between CNF and other polymers improves the swelling performance of the hydrogel, and the mechanical properties are correspondingly enhanced [13–15]. At the same time, if CQDs can be generated synchronously in the preparation process of CNF-based hydrogels, it is expected to achieve the one-step synthesis of CNF based fluorescent hydrogel (CFH), and simplify the preparation process.

Heavy metal pollution in water accumulates through the food chain and causes fatal harm to the human body. Mercury (Hg) is one of the most toxic metals to most organisms, which can effectively interfere with the brain, kidney, immune system, and nervous system at very low concentrations [16]. CQDs can be used as a detecting probe through the quenching or color change to realize rapid quantitative or qualitative analysis of heavy metal ions in aqueous solution, because of their excellent fluorescent effect, high specific surface area, low toxicity, high water solubility and biocompatibility properties [17]. However, due to the high solubility of CQDs in water, it is difficult to separate and recover CQDs after ion detection. In this context, if CQDs can be in-situ formed and immobilized in the hydrogel network, the convenient recovery of CQDs in the mercury ion detection may be achieved.

Based on the above considerations, the formation of CFH and the in-situ synthesis of CQDs were investigated by the hydrothermal method in this study. The preparation process was optimized to obtain CFH with excellent mechanical properties and fluorescence effect. Additionally, the feasibility of CFH in mercury ion detection was also explored. Through achieving rapid one-step preparation of CQD-based CFH and its successful application in mercury ion detection, this study provided a promising method for the development of biocompatible and environmentally friendly fluorescent probes and ion detection materials.

Citric acid monohydrate (CA), anhydrous ethylene diamine (EDA), epichlorohydrin (ECH), and mercury chloride (HgCl2) were purchased from Xilong Scientific Co., Ltd., Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory, Tianjin Yongda Chemical Reagent Development Center, and Guizhou Tongren Chemical Reagent Factory, respectively. Cellulose powder (50 µm) was supplied by Shanghai Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd.

A CNF suspension of in water (~ 4 wt%) was prepared from cellulose powder using a previously reported 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine-1-oxyl (TEMPO) oxidation method [18]. Briefly, the mixture of 10 g of cellulose powder and 500 mL of deionized water was heated and stirred at 38 °C, and 0.2 g TEMPO, 1 g NaBr and 80 g NaClO were successively added, and stirred on a magnetic stirrer. 0.1 mol/L HCl was added dropwise to adjust the reaction pH. Then 0.5 mol/L NaOH was added to keep the pH of the system at about 10, and the reaction lasted for 3 h. Subsequently, the reaction mixture was centrifuged and washed with deionized water, and this process was repeated until the supernatant was neutral. Finally, the treated cellulose was then ultrasonically processed to obtain the CNF suspension.

In a typical run, 3.7 g CNF suspension was mixed with a certain proportion of CA, EDA, and ECH according to the experimental arrangement in Table 1, and it was then treated with ultrasound for 15 min. Subsequently, the mixture was transferred to a 10 mL hydrothermal reactor, and heated at 120 °C for 1–5 h. CFH samples were obtained after the reactor was cooled to room temperature.

In order to remove excess CQDs formed during hydrogel preparation, CFH decolorization was carried out by a water immersion method. The as-prepared CFH samples were immersed in 50 mL deionized water for 3 h to obtain translucent decolorized CFH samples.

In order to determine the selectivity of CFH sample for the Hg2+ detection, decolorized CFH was added into different concentrations of Hg2+ solution (1, 5, 10, 30, and 300 mmol/L). After 10 min of immersion, CFH was observed under sunlight and ultraviolet light (254 and 365 nm), and the fluorescence spectra were collected with fluorescence spectrophotometer.

The fluorescence spectra of the hydrogels were collected using FL3-TCSPC fluorescence spectrophotometer (HORIBA Jobin Yvon, Japan), and the quenching effect of Hg2+ on the hydrogels was estimated through comparing the emission spectra and fluorescence intensity.
The compression test of CFH samples was performed using a ZQ990A mechanical testing machine (Dongguan Zhiqu Precision Instrument Co., Ltd, China) with a loading speed of 1 mm/min.
FTIR spectra of CFH samples were acquired using a Nicolet 6700 infrared spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific Corp, USA) in the 500–4000 cm−1 wavenumber range.

The hydrogel was first frozen in a cold trap at − 55 ℃, and then freeze-dried under vacuum at − 64 ℃ for 24 h. The morphology of the freeze-dried hydrogel sample was observed by an S-3400N scanning electron microscopy (Hitachi, Japan) at 5 kV for secondary electron imaging after being coated with gold.

Fig. 1 presented the fabrication process and the potential formation mechanism of CFH, which was fabricated by a facile one-pot, in-situ hydrothermal method. The in-situ synthesis of CQDs and the formation of the hydrogel system were simultaneously achieved in the process. Particularly, this process made full use of the structural characteristics of CNF, which contained a large number of active hydroxyl groups. Through physical entanglement and hydrogen bonding, a network system of hydrogel skeleton structure was formed [19,20]. The ester bonds were formed through dehydration condensation of the carboxyl group on CA and the hydroxyl group on CNF, and the ether bonds were formed by rings opening of ECH and reacting with the hydroxyl group on CNF, which increased the chemical cross-linking point and improved swelling of hydrogel [21]. In the process of carbon dot formation, both CA and CNF can react with EDA to form CQDs, and the amine groups of EDA were grafted to the surface of carbon dots through the amidation reaction to form nitrogen-doped CQDs having the pseudo-spherical shapes with a graphitic lattice structure [22]. Nitrogen-doped CQDs had a variety of polar groups including hydroxyl, alkyl and carboxyl, which led to surface passivation effect to enhance the fluorescence of CQDs performance and fluorescence quantum yield [23].

As shown in Fig. 2a, with the reaction time increased (1 h, 2 h, 3 h, and 5 h), the degree of carbonization of the hydrogel deepened. Hydrothermal reaction of 2 h led to regular hydrogel shape, promising translucency and excellent fluorescence effect, and the reaction time was set as 2 h in the following experiments.

While keeping the dosage of CNF and CA unchanged, the ratio of CA to EDA was adjusted to 8:1, 8:5, 4:5, 4:9, 1:5, 1:7 and 2:19 respectively by increasing the dosage of EDA. It was observed that the fluorescence intensity of CFH under ultraviolet light was enhanced with the increase of EDA dosage in a certain range (Fig. 2b). According to the transparency and fluorescence intensity of CFH, the optimal molar ratio of CA to EDA was 4:5. The change of reaction time and the ratio of CQD precursors played an important role in the synthesis of CQDs and the crosslinking of hydrogel networks, thus affecting the transparency and morphology of CFH [26].

The effects of the type and amount of crosslinking agent on the properties of CFH were explored by adding GA, silane coupling agent (KH 550) and ECH into the reaction system. As shown in Fig. 2c, ECH was more conducive to hydrogel formation than other crosslinking agents. The good crosslinking effect was mainly attributed to the polymer chain crosslinking reaction of ECH with hydroxyl groups in CNF [27]. These reactions had no influence on the CQD synthesis, thus showing no significant impact on the fluorescence intensity of CFH.

Under the reaction conditions of 120 °C and 2 h, CFH prepared with five different precursor ratios (CFH-2, CFH-14, CFH-15, CFH-16, and CFH-17) showed different fluorescence effects (Fig. 2d), and the comprehensive performance of CFH-2 sample was the best among them. CNF and CA acted as carbon sources in the CQD synthesis, EDA acted as nitrogen source, and all of them were the keys to prepare CFH with high fluorescence intensity. ECH led to regular shape and promising mechanical properties of CFH. At the same time, the CFH-2 sample with ECH as crosslinking agent showed considerable emission fluorescence intensity at 480 nm (Fig. 2e), indicating the excellent crosslinking ability and high fluorescence intensity of this system.

The microstructures of freeze-dried CNF, CFH-16, and CFH-2 were observed by SEM ( Fig. 3a–f). The internal space of CNF showed a relatively loose sheet structure (Fig. 3a and b). After the hydrothermal reaction, the CFH samples exhibited three-dimensional honeycomb structure (Fig. 3c–f), indicating that cellulose formed hydrogels through chemical crosslinking reactions and effectively promoted the development of hierarchical porous network structure. The CNF and other precursors interconnected with each other, and the CFH was easy to absorb and transport water, ions and other molecules. At the same time, the CFH-2 sample showed a more complete 3D network structure than CFH-16 (Fig. 3e and f). This may be mainly because that ECH increased the crosslinking degree, promoted the construction of the three-dimensional structure, and improved the strength of hydrogels [28].

Fig. 3g presented the FT-IR spectra of CFH-2 and CFH-16 prepared with and without the addition of ECH, respectively. Both of them showed the characteristic peak of -OH at 2982–3485 cm−1, indicating the excellent hydrophilicity of CFH. In the spectrum of CFH-2, there was no characteristic peak for the epoxy group at 1352–1217 cm−1, which indicated that the epoxy ring was opened and cross-linked with the hydroxyl group of cellulose when ECH was introduced into the reaction system.

The mechanical properties of five CFH samples were preliminarily tested by placing weights. The results showed that the hydrogel prepared with ECH possessed better compressive strength than that of other CFH samples. The reason may be that ECH participated in the formation of a complete and strong chemical crosslinking network structure in the hydrogel, which can well resist external forces [29,30]. Fig. 3h summarized the compressive strength and Young's modulus of CFH prepared with different ECH additions. ECH, as the crosslinking agent, built a network structure based on the reactions with the group on the surface of CNF, and then enhanced the strength of CFH [31]. However, excessive ECH would reduce the Young's modulus and compressive strength, because the distance between adjacent cross-linking points in the network structure became longer, and the elasticity of the internal cross-linking network of the hydrogel decreased. The movement of the internal molecular chains was promoted during stretching [32,33], which led to an increase in the breaking strain and a decrease in the corresponding mechanical strength.

During the hydrothermal reaction, part of the cellulose reacted with EDA to form CQDs, which deepened the color of CFH and accordingly affected the fluorescence effect of CFH under ultraviolet light. Due to the high solubility of CQDs in water, immersing CFH in distilled water can result in the decolorization of the hydrogel to optimize the fluorescence effect. In a typical decolorization process, CFH was soaked in 50 mL of distilled water for 3 h, and compared with the untreated CFH, the color became transparent after decolorization ( Fig. 4a and b). The fluorescence intensities of CFH samples before and after the decolorization process were analyzed by fluorescence spectrophotometer. As shown in Fig. 4a and b, the intensity of the emission peak of the treated hydrogel slightly decreased, and the peak shifted to the left in the fluorescence spectrum. At 400–440 nm, the emission wavelength of the treated hydrogel was shifted to smaller wavelength, while it moved to larger wavelength at 440–500 nm. The results indicated that CQDs generated by CA and EDA in the hydrogel showed an obvious fluorescent effect, and they can be extracted from the hydrogel to the distilled water through osmosis, making the color of the hydrogel lighter and changing the emission wavelength. At the same time, as shown in Fig. 4c, the soaked water by CFH also had an obvious fluorescence effect, which also confirmed the extraction of CQDs.

In this study, mercury ion detection based on CFH was investigated by studying the effect of different concentrations of Hg2+ solution on CFH fluorescence quenching. First, through performing fluorescence emission of CFH at different excitation wavelengths, it was found that the emission wavelength of 360 nm exhibited the highest excitation intensity, which was selected for subsequent detection ( Fig. 5a). Second, CFH was immersed in different concentrations of Hg2+ solution (0–300 mmol/L) for 10 min, and the fluorescence emission intensity was subsequently determined. As shown in Fig. 5b, with the increase of Hg2+ concentration, the fluorescence quenching effect of the Hg2+ solution on CFH gradually became obvious, and 300 mmol/L Hg2+ solution can result in complete fluorescence quenching. Fig. 5c presented the correlationship between fluorescence emission intensity at 458 nm and mercury ion concentration. As the concentration of Hg2+ increased, the fluorescence intensity of CFH gradually decreased until it was almost completely quenched. The regression equation was as follows:
(1)
y = 192.112e^(-x/63.035)−1.678
where x is the concentration of Hg2+, and R2 is 0.992. The linear correlationship can be achieved in the concentration range of 0–30 mmol/L with a regression equation (R2=0.933) as follows:
(2)
y = −2.1x+183.31

The results indicated that CQDs possessed excellent performance in selective Hg2+ detection. The possible explanation was that Hg2+ had a strong binding force with the N-atoms in the CQDs, and the combination of them led to the decrease of the negative charge on the surface of CQDs. The irradiation of ultraviolet light led to the photoinduced electron transfer, and electrons were transferred from electron-rich CQDs to electron-deficient Hg2+, which caused the fluorescence quenching of CQDs [34,35].

A hydrothermal strategy was developed to achieve simultaneously the one-pot preparation of CNF-based fluorescent hydrogels and the in-situ formation of CQDs. The as-prepared CFHs showed excellent fluorescence properties and promising mechanical strength, and they exhibited a selective fluorescence quenching effect on Hg2+ presence in a wide range of Hg2+ concentration, which provided CFH great application prospects in fluorescent probes and ion detection. The present rapid and efficient preparation strategy of CFH may contribute to the development of sustainable sensing and detection materials and systems.

Fangchao Cheng: Conceptualization, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Shuo Zhang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft, Validation, Investigation. Lveting Zhang: Methodology, Validation, Investigation. Jianping Sun: Methodology, Investigation. Yiqiang Wu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful for the support of the Project Funded by China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2017M620361), the Natural Science Foundation of Guangxi (2020GXNSFAA297028; 2016GXNSFCA380025), and the Scientific Research Foundation of Guangxi University (XGZ150513).
